original_abstract: In Ecuador, community nurses serve as the backbone of health promotion and disease prevention, particularly within rural and marginalized urban settings. This study examines the critical contributions of these professionals in advancing health education, spearheading vaccination campaigns, and managing chronic conditions. Findings indicate that targeted educational interventions significantly foster healthier lifestyles and reduce the incidence of gastrointestinal diseases and childhood malnutrition. Furthermore, nursing-led immunization efforts have successfully expanded coverage in underserved regions, while dedicated follow-up programs have enhanced treatment adherence and overall patient quality of life. Despite these successes, the field faces substantial hurdles, including dilapidated infrastructure, resource scarcity, and limited access to professional development. To optimize the impact of community nursing, this paper advocates for the development of robust public policies, the integration of innovative healthcare technologies, and the fostering of cross-sectoral community partnerships. Addressing these systemic challenges is essential to strengthening the nursing workforce and advancing public health outcomes across Ecuador.
humanized_abstract: Community nurses form the core of health promotion and disease prevention work in Ecuador’s rural and vulnerable urban communities. This paper explores their significant contributions to the planning, implementation and evaluation of a wide range of health education, immunization and chronic disease management activities and the impact that these have had on Ecuadorian communities. The implementation of education activities designed to improve and promote healthy practices, vaccination activities, management of chronic conditions and ongoing monitoring and evaluation of community-based healthcare projects, achieved significant improvements in populations’ health and wellbeing. They include improvements in health practices that improve populations’ health and wellbeing and a reduction in cases of gastro-intestinal diseases and child malnutrition due to the provision of health education in schools. The vaccination programme carried out in the communities by community-based nurses also achieved an increase in the coverage of immunization in some of the most marginalized areas of Ecuador. Finally, the follow-up and on-going monitoring of chronic disease management provided by community-based nurses allowed patients to comply with their medication, hence having a better quality of life. However, the community nursing sector is faced with a plethora of structural barriers that need to be addressed by means of a range of public policies to support the professionals in charge of community-based healthcare that include, among others, the improved infrastructure, better equipment and, in addition, the need for an ongoing professional training of community-based nurses, in order to be competitive, which is one of the priorities of the health sector, by putting the necessary means and mechanisms to foster, improve and innovate the planning, design and implementation of activities such as those aimed at improving population’s health and wellbeing. In the case of Ecuador, the establishment of an innovative framework for community nursing practice should facilitate the improvement of inter-institutional and intersectorial collaboration with local community groups through partnerships at all levels, in a context where the country’s nursing and health systems face significant structural, infrastructure, operational and human resource challenges.
